text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Patient: Margaret Osei-Bonsu
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
Attending: [Attending Oncologist]
Department: Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Follow-up visit for metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Presenting for review of restaging imaging, CA 19-9 trend, and ongoing management following cycle 3 of palliative gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel (Abraxane).

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 67-year-old woman with a well-characterized history of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) who now presents approximately 27 months from her original diagnosis. Her history bears a detailed review for context.

She initially presented at age 66 with a 3-month history of progressive epigastric pain radiating to the back, 14-pound weight loss, and worsening glycemic control on metformin. Workup at that time demonstrated a 3.4 cm hypoattenuating mass in the pancreatic head/uncinate process on triphasic CT, with tumor abutting the SMA over greater than 180 degrees and compressing the SMV lumen by approximately 40%. EUS-guided fine needle biopsy confirmed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (CK7+, CK19+, MUC1+, p53 aberrant overexpression, SMAD4/DPC4 retained). Initial CA 19-9 was 1,200 U/mL. Next-generation sequencing (Tempus xT panel) identified KRAS p.G12V (VAF 42%), TP53 p.R248W (VAF 38%), and CDKN2A homozygous deletion; tumor was microsatellite stable (MSS) and TMB-low (3 mut/Mb). No BRCA1/2, ATM, or PALB2 mutations were identified on either somatic or germline testing.

She was staged as borderline resectable PDAC (clinical T3N0M0, Stage IIA) and was treated in the neoadjuvant setting with modified FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, irinotecan 150 mg/m2 [later reduced to 120 mg/m2 for diarrhea], 5-FU 2,400 mg/m2 continuous infusion over 46 hours, q2 weeks) for 8 cycles. She had a significant CA 19-9 response (1,200 → 85 U/mL) and partial radiographic response (3.4 → 2.6 cm primary tumor; SMA abutment improved from >180 degrees to approximately 120 degrees). She then received long-course concurrent chemoradiation (50.4 Gy in 28 fractions via IMRT with concurrent capecitabine 825 mg/m2 BID on radiation days), which was completed with a brief 3-day break for nausea. Pre-operative restaging CT showed further reduction to 2.3 cm with SMA abutment now estimated at 80-90 degrees.

She underwent pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) approximately 7 months after initial diagnosis at a high-volume HPB center. Final surgical pathology demonstrated a 2.1 cm moderately differentiated PDAC with moderate treatment response (CAP grade 2; approximately 40% viable tumor), R0 resection (closest margin: uncinate/SMA margin at 3 mm), lymphovascular invasion present, perineural invasion present (extensive), and 2 of 18 lymph nodes positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma. Pathologic staging: pT3pN1M0, Stage IIB. The postoperative course was complicated by a grade B pancreatic fistula managed conservatively (octreotide, TPN, prolonged drain), and delayed gastric emptying. She was discharged on postoperative day 18.

Adjuvant therapy with gemcitabine plus capecitabine (ESPAC-4 regimen) was initiated approximately 12 weeks after surgery. Treatment was complicated by grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (requiring capecitabine dose reduction to 75%, subsequently 50%), grade 2 fatigue, grade 1-2 neutropenia, and a re-hospitalization on cycle 3 for a small peripancreatic fluid collection managed conservatively with IV antibiotics and TPN. All 6 cycles were ultimately completed over approximately 9 months (rather than the planned 6 months) with significant dose modifications.

Surveillance imaging was unremarkable at 3 and 6 months post-adjuvant therapy completion (approximately 18 and 21 months from diagnosis), with CA 19-9 remaining within normal limits (19-22 U/mL). However, surveillance CT at approximately 27 months from diagnosis (12 months post-adjuvant therapy completion) identified a new 1.2 cm hypodense hepatic lesion in segment VI, as well as two new small pulmonary nodules (RLL 7 mm, LLL 5 mm), with a rising CA 19-9 of 67 U/mL and CEA of 4.8 ng/mL. MRI liver with gadoxetate disodium (Eovist) confirmed features of metastatic adenocarcinoma in the segment VI lesion (arterial enhancement, portal venous washout, hepatobiliary phase hypointensity). CT-guided core needle biopsy of the hepatic lesion confirmed metastatic adenocarcinoma consistent with pancreatic primary (CK7+, CK19+, SMAD4/DPC4 retained). She was thus restaged as Stage IV metastatic PDAC, representing a recurrence approximately 20 months following surgical resection.

Given prior exposure to oxaliplatin (with residual grade 1-2 peripheral neuropathy at the time of recurrence) and a disease-free interval of greater than 12 months from last chemotherapy, the multidisciplinary tumor board discussed systemic therapy options for the metastatic setting including gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel, FOLFIRINOX rechallenge, and clinical trial enrollment. The patient expressed a strong preference for quality of life and functional independence, and she elected gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel, in part given the concern for additive neurotoxicity with oxaliplatin rechallenge. She initiated gemcitabine 1,000 mg/m2 IV plus nab-paclitaxel 125 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 8, and 15 of a 28-day cycle. After cycle 2, nab-paclitaxel was dose-reduced to 100 mg/m2 due to grade 2 neutropenia and additive peripheral neuropathy. Several day 8 and day 15 doses were held in cycles 3 and 4 for neutropenia; no febrile neutropenia occurred.

Today she presents for review of her cycle 3 restaging CT (obtained last week) and laboratory results. She reports that her fatigue has been grade 2, persistent, and is most bothersome to her. She continues to have some numbness and tingling in her fingertips and toes, roughly grade 2, which she notes is slightly worse compared to her baseline prior to starting this regimen. She has not noticed return of her epigastric or back pain. Appetite has been fair; she is maintaining her weight within approximately 2 lbs of her pre-treatment baseline. She denies nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea at this time. No fever or chills. No jaundice. No new skin changes other than diffuse alopecia (grade 2, expected, consistent with prior hair loss).

Her CA 19-9 drawn last week returned at 45 U/mL, down from 67 U/mL at diagnosis of metastatic recurrence. CEA is 3.9 ng/mL, also trending down from 4.8 ng/mL.

Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast performed last week showed the hepatic segment VI lesion stable at 1.2 cm, the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule stable at 7 mm, and the left lower lobe nodule stable at 5 mm. No new lesions were identified, and there is no evidence of peritoneal spread.

She has had palliative care co-managing her symptom burden and goals of care, and she updated her advance directives at a prior visit. She remains ECOG performance status 1.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Fatigue grade 2, present. Weight stable. No fever. No chills. No night sweats.
Neurologic: Bilateral fingertip and toe paresthesias/numbness, grade 2, stable to very mildly increased. No significant functional impairment with fine motor tasks.
Gastrointestinal: Appetite fair. No nausea. No vomiting. No diarrhea. No constipation. No jaundice. No abdominal pain. No melena or hematochezia. Continues on pancreatic enzyme replacement (pancrelipase) with meals with reasonable stool consistency.
Dermatologic: Diffuse alopecia, grade 2. No new skin breakdown. No hand-foot syndrome at this time (capecitabine not currently on regimen).
Endocrine: Blood glucose management ongoing; continues insulin. Reports glucoses generally in the 130-190 mg/dL range fasting, which she feels is acceptable. Continuing to work with endocrinology as needed.
Cardiopulmonary: No dyspnea, cough, or chest pain. No lower extremity edema.
Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain. No joint pain.
Genitourinary: No dysuria or urinary frequency changes.
Psychosocial: Patient reports feeling "reasonably at peace" with her situation. Engaged with palliative care. Family support described as strong.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/74 mmHg, heart rate 76 bpm, respiratory rate 16, temperature 98.3 degrees F, oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Weight: [on file, stable]. BMI approximately 23.8 kg/m2.

General: Alert and oriented, pleasant and cooperative. Well-appearing in no acute distress. Alopecia present.
HEENT: Anicteric sclerae. Mucous membranes moist. No oral mucositis. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical or supraclavicular regions.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Well-healed midline surgical scar. No hepatomegaly appreciated on percussion or palpation. No splenomegaly. No ascites. No palpable abdominal masses. Bowel sounds present and normoactive.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. Distal extremities warm. Decreased sensation to light touch in distal fingertips and toes bilaterally, symmetric, consistent with known chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy. No obvious ataxia on brief standing observation.
Skin: Alopecia noted. No palmar-plantar erythema. No new rashes.
Neurologic: Alert and oriented x3. No focal neurologic deficits. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact.
Lymph nodes: No palpable axillary, inguinal, or other peripheral lymphadenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
CBC: WBC 4.2 x 10^9/L (ANC 2.1 x 10^9/L), hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL (mild anemia, stable from prior), platelets 218 x 10^9/L.
CMP: Sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 4.0 mEq/L, creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60), total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL, ALT 28 U/mL, AST 31 U/mL, alkaline phosphatase 94 U/mL, albumin 3.6 g/dL.
Fasting glucose: 162 mg/dL (suboptimal, consistent with ongoing post-pancreatectomy endocrine insufficiency on insulin).
HbA1c: 7.9% (improved from prior 9.2% at diagnosis, reasonable for her clinical situation).
Tumor markers: CA 19-9 45 U/mL (down from 67 U/mL at start of this regimen); CEA 3.9 ng/mL (down from 4.8 ng/mL).

---

IMAGING RESULTS
Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (obtained approximately 1 week prior to this visit, after 3 cycles of gemcitabine/Abraxane):
- The hepatic segment VI lesion is unchanged at 1.2 cm. No new hepatic lesions.
- Right lower lobe pulmonary nodule unchanged at 7 mm. Left lower lobe nodule unchanged at 5 mm. No new pulmonary lesions.
- No evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis. No ascites.
- Post-Whipple surgical anatomy stable. No new abdominal masses. No lymphadenopathy.
- Overall interpretation: stable disease with no new sites of involvement.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, CDKN2A deleted, MSS, TMB-low), currently on palliative first-line gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel (Abraxane).
   - Restaging CT after 3 cycles demonstrates stable disease: the known hepatic segment VI metastasis and bilateral pulmonary nodules are all unchanged in size, with no new sites of disease identified. CA 19-9 is trending down (67 → 45 U/mL) and CEA is also declining (4.8 → 3.9 ng/mL). This is an encouraging and reassuring response to date.
   - The patient and I reviewed the imaging in detail today. I emphasized that "stable" disease on treatment is a favorable outcome for metastatic pancreatic cancer, and the declining tumor markers add to our confidence that the disease is being held in check. She understood and expressed relief.
   - We discussed the goals of therapy: disease control, maintenance of quality of life, and symptom management. She reiterated her preference to continue treatment as long as it is working and she is tolerating it.
   - Plan: Continue gemcitabine 1,000 mg/m2 IV plus nab-paclitaxel 100 mg/m2 IV (reduced dose) on days 1, 8, and 15 of a 28-day cycle. Next cycle to begin in approximately 2 weeks (cycle 4, day 1). We will continue to hold day 8/15 doses per our standard neutropenia guidelines (hold if ANC <1.0 x 10^9/L on day of treatment).
   - Next restaging CT planned after approximately every 3 cycles (approximately cycle 6).
   - No currently actionable genomic alterations identified on prior NGS (KRAS G12V; no NTRK fusions, no RET alterations, no FGFR2 alterations, no NRG1 fusions, no BRCA/ATM/PALB2 mutations). We will continue to monitor the landscape for emerging KRAS-targeted therapy options (KRAS G12V inhibitors and pan-KRAS programs currently in trials; will revisit eligibility if disease progresses).
   - Clinical trial enrollment remains an open option at time of progression.

2. Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN), grade 2.
   - The patient has cumulative neurotoxicity from prior oxaliplatin (mFOLFIRINOX) and now ongoing nab-paclitaxel exposure. Current symptoms are grade 2 (numbness and tingling bilaterally in distal fingertips and toes, no functional impairment, no gait instability).
   - We have already dose-reduced nab-paclitaxel to 100 mg/m2. I counseled that if neuropathy worsens to grade 3 (functional impairment, activities of daily living affected), we would hold nab-paclitaxel and reassess.
   - Recommended duloxetine 30 mg daily for 1 week then increasing to 60 mg daily for CIPN symptom management (consistent with ASCO guideline data for paclitaxel-associated neuropathy). Prescription sent to pharmacy.
   - Physical therapy referral placed for balance and gait assessment.

3. Myelosuppression, grade 2 neutropenia.
   - ANC today 2.1 x 10^9/L; adequate for proceeding with next cycle on schedule. We will continue per protocol, holding day 8/15 doses if ANC is insufficient on day of treatment.
   - Mild anemia (Hgb 10.6 g/dL) is stable and asymptomatic. Continue iron supplementation. No indication for erythropoiesis-stimulating agents at this time.
   - G-CSF (pegfilgrastim or filgrastim per protocol) will be continued.

4. Post-pancreatectomy exocrine and endocrine insufficiency.
   - Continuing pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (pancrelipase, dose per prior GI/endocrine recommendations). Stool consistency reported as acceptable.
   - Post-surgical diabetes continues on insulin regimen. Fasting glucose today 162 mg/dL; HbA1c 7.9%. Given the competing oncologic management priorities, this is an acceptable range. Endocrinology co-management continues.

5. Nutritional status and weight management.
   - Weight is stable, which is encouraging in the context of metastatic pancreatic cancer. She is meeting with our oncology dietitian.
   - Appetite remains fair. Encourage small frequent meals and high-calorie, high-protein diet.
   - No evidence of cachexia at this time.

6. Fatigue, grade 2.
   - Likely multifactorial: chemotherapy effect, mild anemia, underlying malignancy, post-surgical changes.
   - Continue supportive measures: energy conservation strategies (discussed with patient), adequate sleep hygiene, gentle physical activity as tolerated.
   - Palliative care team engaged and co-managing.

7. Psychosocial/goals of care.
   - Patient remains ECOG performance status 1. She is engaged in her care and has a strong support network.
   - Advance directives have been updated. Palliative care team continues to provide ongoing support.
   - She expressed interest in remaining as functionally independent as possible and spending quality time with her family. We will continue to align treatment decisions with these stated goals.
   - No referral to hospice at this time; patient is on active palliative therapy with disease control.

Return to clinic in 2 weeks for cycle 4 day 1 of gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel and laboratory assessment. CBC with differential to be drawn on day 1 prior to treatment. Patient instructed to contact the oncology triage line for any fever >=38 degrees C, severe nausea or vomiting, inability to maintain oral hydration, significant worsening of neuropathy, or any other concerning new symptoms.

Time spent with patient today: approximately 45 minutes, including extended discussion of imaging results, treatment goals, and symptom management.

Electronically signed by: [Attending Medical Oncologist]
Dictated/finalized: [Date to be added programmatically]
